meaning: I have received all of your last transmission.
operationalMeaning: Roger acknowledges receipt — it does not mean approval, wilco, or readback.
whenUsed: To confirm information received when no readback or action is required.
whyUsed: Closes the loop on ATC information without cluttering the frequency with repetition.

realExamples.text: ANAC 123, traffic twelve o'clock, five miles, opposite direction.
realExamples.speaker: Approach

realExamples.text: Wind three three zero at one five, runway three six.
realExamples.speaker: Tower

icaoQuestions: When should you use roger instead of a full readback?
Why is roger wrong as a reply to a clearance requiring readback?
commonMistakes: Saying roger instead of reading back a clearance.
Using roger to mean yes — use affirm for yes/no questions.
Brazilian pilots sometimes say 'received' — roger is standard.

compareTerms.note: Wilco adds compliance; roger is receipt only.
Affirm answers yes/no; roger acknowledges information.

didYouKnow: Roger comes from the phonetic 'R' for 'received' in legacy radio procedure.
captainStory: Approach passed traffic we could not see in haze — 'Roger, looking' bought time until the traffic appeared at ten o'clock.
